title: Sections and landmarks

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific HTML: Semantic and Accessible Web Documents draft for Sections and landmarks; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Native header, nav, main, aside, and footer elements expose page regions and support landmark navigation when used for their intended roles.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A page has one main content region and labels two nav regions as Primary and Course topics. Your goal is to structure a page with one clear main region and meaningful labeled navigation and sections. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Standards-mode document skeleton; Heading hierarchy; Lists and grouped content. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: Native header, nav, main, aside, and footer elements expose page regions and support landmark navigation when used for their intended roles. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Multiple landmarks of one type may need accessible labels, and header or footer landmark behavior depends on nesting context. This draft therefore rejects: Every div should be replaced with a landmark element to maximize accessibility.

includes: Use header, nav, main, article, section, aside and footer when their semantic roles apply.
Structure a page with one clear main region and meaningful labeled navigation and sections.
excludes: Multiple landmarks of one type may need accessible labels, and header or footer landmark behavior depends on nesting context.

outcomes: Structure a page with one clear main region and meaningful labeled navigation and sections.

examples.title: Sections and landmarks: concrete case
examples.situation: A page has one main content region and labels two nav regions as Primary and Course topics.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A page has one main content region and labels two nav regions as Primary and Course topics.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Use landmarks only for genuine page regions, keep one primary main region, and label repeated landmark types distinctly.
examples.result: The result follows because Use landmarks only for genuine page regions, keep one primary main region, and label repeated landmark types distinctly.

examples.title: Sections and landmarks: boundary case
examples.situation: Multiple landmarks of one type may need accessible labels, and header or footer landmark behavior depends on nesting context.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Start from the documented boundary: Multiple landmarks of one type may need accessible labels, and header or footer landmark behavior depends on nesting context.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: Every div should be replaced with a landmark element to maximize accessibility.
examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: Use landmarks only for genuine page regions, keep one primary main region, and label repeated landmark types distinctly.
